coredata.dc:title: Pyrimidine-5-carbonitrile derivatives as sprout for CQDs proveniences: Antitumor and anti-inflammatory potentiality
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed, Hanan B.
Mikhail, Mary M.
Abdallah, Amira E.M.
El-Shahat, Mahmoud
Emam, Hossam E.
coredata.dc:description: A comparative study is proposed to show the effect of variation in the heteroatoms in the main skeleton of CQDs proveniences, on their affinity for nucleation of CQDs, as anti-inflammatory and anticancer drugs. Heterocyclic-based CQDs sprout was successfully exploited for preparation of three CQDs proveniences, named as; 2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-6,11-dihydro-4H-pyrimido[2,1-b] quinazoline-3-carbonitrile (compound A), 2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3]oxazine-3-carbonitrile (compound S) and 2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3] thiazine-3-carbonitrile (compound T). Chemical formulas of CQDs proveniences & CQDs were verified via FTIR, 1HNMR, 13CNMR & XRD. Particle size of TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs & T-CQDs were estimated to be 3.7 ± 1.4, 4.6 ± 1.6, 5.9 ± 1.6 nm and 3.0 ± 1.3 nm, respectively. All of CQDs proveniences & CQDs were examined for their affinity as anti-inflammatory drugs via Griess assay. CQDs ingrained from TM (TM-CQDs) were detected with the highest NO inhibition% by increasing its concentration from 10 up to 100 μM to be 40 % to 89 %, respectively. Moreover, their anti-tumor performance against MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cell line was approved via sulforhodamine B assay, whereas, IC50 was evaluated for TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs and T-CQDs to be 38.16, 36.09, 100 and 100 μg/ml, respectively.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A comparative study is proposed to show the effect of variation in the heteroatoms in the main skeleton of CQDs proveniences, on their affinity for nucleation of CQDs, as anti-inflammatory and anticancer drugs. Heterocyclic-based CQDs sprout was successfully exploited for preparation of three CQDs proveniences, named as; 2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-6,11-dihydro-4H-pyrimido[2,1-b] quinazoline-3-carbonitrile (compound A), 2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3]oxazine-3-carbonitrile (compound S) and 2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3] thiazine-3-carbonitrile (compound T). Chemical formulas of CQDs proveniences & CQDs were verified via FTIR, 1HNMR, 13CNMR & XRD. Particle size of TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs & T-CQDs were estimated to be 3.7 ± 1.4, 4.6 ± 1.6, 5.9 ± 1.6 nm and 3.0 ± 1.3 nm, respectively. All of CQDs proveniences & CQDs were examined for their affinity as anti-inflammatory drugs via Griess assay. CQDs ingrained from TM (TM-CQDs) were detected with the highest NO inhibition% by increasing its concentration from 10 up to 100 μM to be 40 % to 89 %, respectively. Moreover, their anti-tumor performance against MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cell line was approved via sulforhodamine B assay, whereas, IC50 was evaluated for TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs and T-CQDs to be 38.16, 36.09, 100 and 100 μg/ml, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Mortality percent that results from the death effects of various diseases are reported to be highly affected by the side effects of different drugs, that are still currently administered for the patients [1]. Most of the anti-tumor drugs that are currently exploited have shown limited effectiveness and the administration of such types of drugs might result in some of side effects like the kidney failure and heart diseases [1]. In the tumor treatment, the poor selectivity results in that the anti-tumor drugs act in targeting the healthy cells during the treatment, to be reflected in the toxic side effectiveness [2]. The expensiveness of the anti-tumor drugs decreases the number of patients that involved and successfully complete the treatment [3].

On the other hand, in case of the tissue injury, the infection with pathogens or exposing to toxic drugs, the cells respond by mediation of the immunity mechanism for assisting with tissue repairing and pathogen destructing [4]. Such physiological response is identified as inflammation [5,6]. The inflammation that is reflected in heat swelling redness, pain and abnormal tissue functionalization, is described as the acute inflammation [7,8]. However, tissue damaging that occur during the prolonged inflammation, is known as a chronic inflammation [4,5].

Drugs that are exploited in large scale for the treatment of inflammation are belonged to the class of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) [9]. The affinity for inhibiting the denaturation of proteins is the main action of anti-inflammatory drugs (such as, acetylsalicylic acid, ibufenac, ibuprofen and dichlofenac) [10]. Although the anti-inflammatory activity displayed by NSAIDs showed promising action, but the exploitation of such type of drugs might result in harmful side effects as gastric ulcers, heart problems, kidney and liver damaging [4,8,9,11]. The side effects are attributed to the exploitation of NSAIDs encouraged the researchers interested in such field of study to investigate new anti-inflammatory drugs with higher safety in order to tackle the contributing effects of the inflammation in initiation of different cancer diseases [12]. Additionally, tissue damage is owing to the chronic inflammation that might lead to cardiovascular diseases and rheumatoid arthritis [5,10,13]. Therefore, it is essential to investigate different medical drug that alleviates the un-preferable consequences of inflammation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as biocompatible/ nontoxic spherical nanoparticles (1–10 nm) are advantageous with thermal stability & high affinity for adsorption [14], superior electrical conductivity [15], anti-corrosive performance [16], metal markers [17], ultraviolet-protection [18], plant nourishment [19] and high catalytic potency [20,21]. Owing to the biocompatibility and non-toxicity of CQDs, they found a wide scaled applicability in biomedical purposes [22]. Some of researching reports were interestingly considered with studying cytotoxic effects of CQDs on various cancer cell lines like, Hep-2 [23], HeLa [24] and A549 [25,26]. Moreover, Xu et al, were studied the affinity of carbon dots ingrained from aspirin, to be sequentially applicable for bioimaging and as anti-inflammatory drugs [27–31]. Recently, modified biopolymers based starch and chitosan were used for production of anticancer and antiviral CQDs [32,33].

Herein, a comparative study is proposed in order to show the effect of variation in the heteroatoms that sharing in the main skeleton of the aromatic nucleus for CQDs proveniences, to study their affinity in nucleation of CQDs as anti-inflammatory and anticancer drugs. Three different heterocyclic compounds named as; “2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-6,11-dihydro-4H-pyrimido[2,1-b] quinazoline-3-carbonitrile” (compound A), “2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3]oxazine-3-carbonitrile” (compound S) and “2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3]thiazine-3-carbonitrile” (compound S). Sequentially, TM, A, T & S compounds were exploited as CQDs proveniences, whereas, the ingrained CQDs were applied as concurrent anti-inflammatory/anti-tumor drugs. The chemical formulas for CQDs proveniences (TM, A, T & S) and the ingrained CQDs were verified with FTIR, 1HNMR, 13CNMR & XRD. Afterward, the nucleated CQDs were characterized via TEM and Zetasizer for estimation of particle and investigate their topography and geometrical shapes. Fluorescence sensitivity was investigated for all of CQDs proveniences (TM, A, T & S) and the ingrained CQDs. CQDs proveniences and CQDs were all examined for their affinity as anti-inflammatory drugs via Griess assay. Moreover, their anti-tumor performance against MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cell line was approved via sulforhodamine B assay.

Ethyl cyanoacetate (CNCH2CO2C2H5), 2,5-dimethoxy benzaldehyde (2,5-(CH3O)2C6H3CHO), thiourea ((NH2)2CS), anthranilic acid (2-(NH2)C6H4COOH), Salicylic acid (2-(HO)C6H4COOH), and thio-salicylic acid (2-(HS)C6H4COOH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company assay ≥ 97 %. Sodium metal, ethyl alcohol (C2H5OH), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. All materials were used without further purification.

A mixture of 1 mmol/L “2,5-dimethoxybenzaldehyde”, 1 mmol/L of “thiourea” and 1 mmol/L of “ethyl cyanoacetate” in NaOEt was stirred for 60 min at room temperature. The reaction liquor was poured in cold water to be sequentially acidified with HCl; filtered, dried for recrystallization using EtOH to produce TM. The estimated value of yield percent was 92 % and melting point was detected to be 166–168 °C. IR spectral data (KBr, ν, cm−1): 3204, 3113 (2NH); 2256 (CN); 1720 (CO). 1H NMR spectral mapping (DMSO‑d 6, δ ppm): 3.77 (s, 3H, OCH3), 3.80 (s, 3H, OCH3), 6.68–6.82 (m, 3H, Ar-H), 10.11 (s, 1H, NH; D2O exchangeable), 11.70 (s, 1H, NH; D2O exchangeable). 13C NMR spectral results (DMSO, δ ppm): 181.88 (CS), 162.53 (CO), 149.70 (Ar-C), 143.23 (Ar-C) 141.35(Ar-C), 139.67(Ar-C), 137.96 (Ar-C), 134.31 (Ar-C), 128.56 (Ar-C), 128.00 (Ar-C), 116.73 (CN), 55.99 (OCH3), 55.23 (OCH3).

A mixture of 1 mmol/L TM and 1 mmol/L “anthranilic acid”, in 2 % NaOEt was refluxed for one day. The reaction liquor was cooled, poured onto cold water to be sequentially acidified with HCl, filtered, washed with water, dried for eventual recrystallization using EtOH to produce compound A. The yield percentage was 90 % and melting point was 153–155 °C. IR spectral data showed that (KBr, ν, cm−1): 3225 (NH); 2219 (CN); 1735 (CO); 1700 (CO). 1H NMR spectrum (DMSO‑d 6, δ ppm): δ 7.39 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 7.26 (s, 1H, NH; D2O exchangeable), 7.01–7.03 (m, 4H, Ar-H), 6.66 (td, J = 7.4, 1.1 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 6.53 (dd, J = 8.3, 1.0 Hz, 1H Ar-H), 3.86 (s, 3H, OCH3), 3.83 (s, 3H, OCH3).13C NMR (DMSO, δ ppm): 178.99 (CO), 173.37 (CO), 159.64 (Ar-C), 153.19 (Ar-C), 149.77 (Ar-C), 146.81 (Ar-C), 142.82 (Ar-C), 140.34 (Ar-C), 135.07 (Ar-C), 131.42 (Ar-C), 128.87 (Ar-C), 125.67 (Ar-C), 125.12(Ar-C), 122.43(Ar-C), 120.29 (Ar-C), 113.98 (CN), 55.77(OCH3), 54.02(OCH3).

A mixture of 1 mmol/L TM and 1 mmol/L salicylic acid, in 2 % NaOEt was refluxed for one day. The reaction colloid was cooled, poured onto cold water to be subsequentially acidified with HCl, filtered, washed with H2O, dried to be finally recrystallized from EtOH to produce compound S. The yield percent was estimated to be 81 % and melting point was 171–173 °C. IR spectral data revealed that (KBr, ν, cm−1): 2214 (CN); 1715 (CO), 1698 (CO). 1H NMR spectrum (DMSO‑d 6, δ ppm): 8.41–8.46 (m, 4H, Ar-H), 6.95 (d, J = 2.4 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 6.81 (dd, J = 8.2, 2.0 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 6.59 (dd, J = 8.4, 2.4 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 3.86 (s, 3H, OCH3), 3.79 (s, 3H, OCH3). 13C NMR (DMSO, δ ppm): 172.77 (CO), 167.59 (CO), 160.13 (Ar-C), 151.75 (Ar-C), 147.87 (Ar-C), 146.91 (Ar-C), 141.84 (Ar-C), 140.01 (Ar-C), 132.69 (Ar-C), 132.13 (Ar-C), 129.16 (Ar-C), 126.89 (Ar-C), 125.48 (Ar-C), 123.43 (Ar-C), 119.07 (Ar-C), 114.13 (CN), 56.52 (OCH3), 56.13 (OCH3).

A mixture of 1 mmol/L TM and 1 mmol/L “thio-salicylic acid”, in 2 % NaOEt was refluxed for one day. The reaction mixture was cooled, poured on cold water to be acidified with HCl, filtered, washed with water, dried to be recrystallized from EtOH to give compound T. The yield 74 %; m.p. 182–184 °C. IR spectral data (KBr, ν, cm−1): 2217 (CN); 1722 (CO), 1695 (CO). 1H NMR spectrum (DMSO‑d 6, δ ppm): 7.35 (d, J = 8.1 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 7.24–7.28 (m, 2H, Ar-H), 7.02–7.06 (m, 2H, Ar-H), 6.68 (td, J = 7.4, 1.0 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 6.55 (dd, J = 8.3, 1.0 Hz, 1H, Ar-H), 3.85 (s, 3H, OCH3), 3.83 (s, 3H, OCH3). 13C NMR (DMSO, δ ppm): 177.69 (CO), 171.56 (CO), 167.71 (Ar-C), 161.53 (Ar-C), 156.26 (Ar-C), 151.82 (Ar-C), 150.14 (Ar-C), 145.60 (Ar-C), 138.28 (Ar-C), 137.32 (Ar-C), 131.83 (Ar-C), 129.19 (Ar-C), 122.96 (Ar-C), 122.76 (Ar-C), 122.40 (Ar-C), 120.01 (Ar-C), 114.62 (CN), 56.75 (OCH3), 56.30 (OCH3).

Under the solvothermal conditions, CQDs were successively ingrained from CQDs proveniences (TM, A, S & T compounds). In this procedure, 1 g of each compound was solubilized in 100 mL of DMF, to be transferred in a vertical solvothermal autoclavable cup and placed in oven at 210 °C for 6 h. the solutions were kept for cooling at room temperature for dialysis with DMF using pur-A-lyzer dialysis kits (MWCO 6–8 kDa from Sigma-Aldrich) for one day to obtain monodispersed/ultra-filtered CQDs, to be ready for analysis and characterization.

Infrared spectra were recorded on Perkin-Elmer 1600 FTIR (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA) discs. NMR spectra were determined on a Jeol-Ex-300 NMR spectrometer (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) and chemical shifts were expressed as part per million; (δ values, ppm) against TMS as an internal reference, National Research Center, Cairo, Egypt. 1H and 13C chemical shifts were referred to the solvent signal (DMSO‑d6 ) at 2.50 and 39.52 ppm, respectively. Data are presented as follows: chemical shift, integration, multiplicity (s = singlet, d = doublet, dd = doublet of doublet, td = triplet of doublet) and coupling constants in Hertz (Hz).

The absorbance of CQDs proveniences and the implanted CQDs was estimated at 250–750 nm by using a spectrophotometer “Cary 100 UV–VIS, UV–Vis-NIR Systems, from Agilent”. The topography and size average of the implanted CQDs were estimated using JEOL-JEM-1200; Japan (high-resolution transmission electron microscope). Average size of CQDs was estimated with 4 pi analysis software, USA. Infrared spectral data were obtained by Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometry. Absorbance also detected at 500–4000 cm−1 “15-point smoothing, 4 cm−1 resolution, and 64-time scanning with a scanning rate of 2 mm·sec −1″. The spectral data of nuclear magnetic resonance (1HNMR and 13CNMR) were estimated with ”Jeol‐Ex‐300 NMR spectrometry, JEOL–Japan“. Photoluminescence of CQDs in the ultraviolet–visible range was monitored via spectro-fluorometry (JASCO FP8300). The results were detected at room temperature (excitation at 340 nm).

Digital images of CQDs proveniences (TM, S, A &T) and the implanted CQDs were taken using ultra-violet lamp “output 4 W and input 220 V AC”. Photos for samples captured with excitation at 325 nm using a cell phone camera (Oppo A31). Absorbance of CQDs proveniences and the ingrained CQDs were estimated using “FLAME-S-UV–VIS spectrometry”, USA.Fluorescence was also followed up using “Jasco FP-6500 spectrofluorometer, 150 W Xenon lamp, with concave holographic grating of 1800 grooves/mm emission monochromator and coupled with a second photomultiplier; Japan”. The prepared colloids were excited at 360 nm, and the emission spectral data were collected at ambient conditions.

Cell viability assay is performed for detection the physiological and physical changes that occurred in cells as a result of extracellular stimulation [34]. Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay is a colorimetric test that depends on the fluorescent sulforhodamine B dye which binds to basic amino acids in the cell. SRB assay is known to be advantageous with cost effectiveness, accuracy and high sensitivity. Via the colorimetric test, the total mass of protein can be calculated for identification of the cells number. In this approach, SRB assay was performed for approving the antitumor affinity of the TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs and T-CQDs against MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cell line. MCF-7 cell line was obtained from Nawah Scientific Inc., (Mokatam, Cairo, Egypt). Cells were maintained in DMEM media supplemented with 100 mg/mL of streptomycin, 100 units/mL of penicillin and 10 % of humidified/heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum and five percent of (v/v) CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Aliquots of 100 μL cell (5x10^3 cells) were suspended in 96-well plates to be incubated for one day. Cells were treated with another aliquot of 100 μL medium containing the prepared TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs and T-CQDs with different concentrations. After three days, cells were fixed by replacing medium with 150 μL of 10 % trichloroacetic acid to be incubated at 4 °C for one hour. Trichloroacetic acid removed, while the cells were washed for 5 times with distilled H2O. Aliquots of 70 μL SRB solution (0.4 % w/v) were added for incubation in dark at ambient conditions for 10 min. Afterword, the prepared plates washed for 3 times with 1 % CH3COOH for air-drying overnight. Subsequentially, 150 μL of TRIS (10 mM) was added to dissolve protein-bonded to SRB dye. Absorption was detected at 540 nm via “BMGLABTECH®- FLUOstar Omega microplate reader, Ortenberg, Germany”.

RAW264.7 cells, “Murine macrophages, ATCC®” were maintained in “Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium, DMEM, Corning, USA” supplemented with ten percent of “fetal bovine serum”, 100 U/mL of “penicillin”, 100 µg/mL of “streptomycin sulphate” and 2 mM of “L-glutamine” in a humidified incubator with five percent of carbon dioxide. Firstly, cells washed with “buffered phosphate saline” using sterile, the flasks were scrapped off (SPL, Spain). Stock of “RAW 264.7 cells, 0.5 × 106 cells/mL” were seeded in “96-well microwell plates” for overnight incubation. Afterword, “non-induced triplicate wells” were treated in a medium with samples vehicle, “DMSO, 0.1 % v/v”. Inflammation group of triplicate wells were treated with the inflammation inducer “lipopolysaccharide (LPS) as 100 ng/mL” with a complete culturing medium, containing 0.1 % of dimethyl sulfoxide, v/v. Samples of “triplicate wells” were treated with screen concentrations of 10 and100 µM of sample solubilized in dimethyl sulfoxide and for dilution in LPS, whereas, the concentration of dimethyl sulfoxide was 0.1 %, by volume. 0.25 mM of Indo (Indomethacin) was exploited as blank sample. After incubation for one day, Griess assay [35] was applied for determination of nitrous oxide “NO”. Equal amounts of Griess drug and culture supernatant were mixed to be incubated at ambient conditions for 10 min to give colored diazonium salt and for estimation of absorption at 540 nm on “Tecan Sunrise™ microplate reader” (Austria). NO Inhibition percentage of tested compounds was estimated in accordance to LPS-induced inflammatory groups, normalized to the cell viability evaluated via “Alamar Blue™ reduction assay” [36]. The concentration that inhibits 50 % of LPS-induced NO releasing was statistically estimated on GraphPad Prism software via the curve fitting to a non-linear regression modeling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TM, A, S & T
ce:section.ce:para:
A schematic diagram for the mechanism of TM, A, S & T compounds' synthesis is presented in Fig. 1 . Whereas, spectral mapping data for all the prepared compounds are also plotted in Figs. 2, 3 & 4
. From the plotted data it could be depicted that, refluxing a mixture of “2,5-dimethoxybenzaldehyde”, “thiourea”, and “ethyl cyanoacetate”, produced 2-thioxopyrimidine-5-carbonitrile derivative (TM) as a CQDs sprout [37]. Pyrimidine-5-carbonitrile (TM) was allowed to react with 2-substituted benzoic acids such as anthranilic acid, salicylic acid and thio-salicylic acid in basic media, to produce the corresponding fused pyrimidine derivatives A, S and T; respectively.

1H NMR spectrum for TM shows signals at δ (ppm) = 3.77, 3.80 for two (OCH3) groups as two singlet signals, 6.68–6.82 for three aromatic protons, and two singlet signals at 10.11, 11.70 for two cyclic (NH) groups. While, 13C NMR spectrum displays signals at δ = 181.88 ppm attributed to the cyclic thioxo group (CS), 162.53 ppm refers to the cyclic carbonyl group (CO), 116.73 ppm for cyano group (CN), 55.99 and 55.23 ppm for two (OCH3) groups besides signal attributed to Sp2 carbons at 149.70–128.00 ppm. For A compound, 1H NMR spectrum shows new multiple peaks at 7.01–7.03 ppm, attributed to the new formed fused aromatic ring. 13C- NMR spectrum displays two characteristic signals for carbonyl groups at 178.99 and 173.37 ppm with a signal characteristic for aromatic carbon at 159.64–120.29 ppm, besides, signal at 113.98, 55.77, and 54.02 ppm are assigned for (CN), and two (OCH3) groups, respectively.

Whereas, 1H NMR spectrum of S compound displays new peaks at 8.41–8.46 for new four Ar-H. 13C NMR represents signals at 172.77 and 167.59 ppm for two (CO) groups, 160.13 –119.07 for (Ar-C), 114.13 ppm for (CN), 56.52 ppm for (OCH3) and at 56.13 ppm for (OCH3). However, 1H NMR spectrum of T compound shows two multiple peaks at 7.24–7.28 and 7.02–7.06 ppm for new four aromatic protons. 13C NMR represents observable signals at 177.69 and 171.56 ppm for two (CO) groups, 167.71–120.01 ppm for Sp2 carbons, 114.62 ppm for (CN) group, 56.75 and at 56.30 ppm for two (OCH3) groups. So it could be summarized that, The appearance of new signals attributed to the carbonyl group in 13C NMR spectra beside the appearance of the new signal peak attributed to four new aromatic protons elucidate the structure for new hetero-functionalized mixed and non-mixed ring system A, S and T.

The infrared (IR) spectra that are plotted in Fig. 4a for the new pyrimidine derivatives show that; TM is characterized with absorption band characteristics for two (NH), (CN), (CO) and (CS) groups at 3204, 3113, 2256, 1720 and 1180 cm−1 respectively. (A) can be identified as a non-mixed heterocyclic ring system; where IR spectrum displays characteristic bands for (NH), (CN), two (CO) and (CN) groups at 3225; 2219; 1735, 1700 and 1640 cm−1; respectively. However, IR spectrum for (S) shows the characteristic band at 2214, 1715, 1698 and 1640 cm−1 for (CN); (CO), (CO) and (CN) groups, respectively. While, IR spectrum for (T) displays characteristic bands at 2217, 1722, 1695 and1640 cm−1 for (CN), two (CO) and (CN) groups. In order to show the change in the functional groups and affirm the desirable derivatization, a comparable overview for IR spectra of all the prepared compounds could be pointed out as follows; i) the characteristic band of CS at 1180 cm−1 that was estimated for TM was disappeared in the others, to affirm its successive contribution for successive cyclization, ii) a significant band at 1690–1640 cm−1 was estimated for A, S & T compounds that is corresponding to CN, iii) band assigned for NH was disappeared for S& T compounds, iv) corresponding band for CO at 1200 cm−1 was only observed in case of S compound spectrum, v) a broad peak at 3600–3700 cm−1 for intermolecular hydrogen bonding was observed for T & S compounds.

XRD analysis for TM and the as-synthesized A, S &T compounds were plotted in Fig. 4b, to show that, three diffraction bands for TM compound were detected at 2θ = 6.8°, 10.4° 25.2° and 26.4°. After its exploitation of implanting the desired A, S & T compounds, all-diffraction bands at 2θ = 6.8°, 10.4° and 26.4° were completely disappeared. This meaning that, the crystalline structure was completely differed due to conversion to CQDs proveniences with different chemical structures. However, for A compound, the diffraction band at 2θ = 25.2° became more intense, but for S compound, no diffraction peaks were estimated, to assign for the amorphous structure of S compound. But, in case of T compound, new intense band was observed at 2θ = 22.9° beside three tiny band at 2θ = 22.9°, 27.7° and 35.8°. the diffraction spectra sign for the crystalline structure of T compound.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis and characterization of the ingrained CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were successfully ingrained via a solvothermal technique from TM and CQDs proveniences (A, S & T compounds). The mechanism of CQD synthesis could be predicted as follows; TM and CQDs proveniences were supposed to be hydrolyzed and fragmented under solvothermal conditions, for sequential polymerization, aromatization, and oxidation. Whereas, controllable sized and shaped CQDs as aromatic graphite sheets were successfully implanted and decorated with oxygen-, sulfur-, and nitrogen-functional groups [38–42].

Geometrical features and topography were investigated for CQDs ingrained from TM (TM-CQDs, Fig. 5 a), A (A-CQDs, Fig. 5b), S (S-CQDs, Fig. 5c) & T (T-CQDs, Fig. 5d), via the TEM microscopic analysis from which their size distribution was estimated. The microscopic images show that, the synthesized CQDs were homogeneous/well dispersed, spherical shaped with size average lesser than 10 nm. TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs & T-CQDs were characterized with size distribution of 3.7 ± 1.4 nm, 4.6 ± 1.6 nm, 5.9 ± 1.6 nm & 3.0 ± 1.3 nm, meanwhile, T-CQDs was exhibited with the smallest particle size. For more confirmation, Fig. 6 represents the size distribution of all the prepared compounds before and after their exploitation for ingraining CQDs, to show that, all the prepared compounds were exhibited with size distribution in range of 200 nm up to 1100 nm, whereas, under solvothermal conditions, the size distribution was significantly diminished to be in range of 2–13 nm, whereas, TM-CQDs, A-CQDs, S-CQDs & T-CQDs were estimated with size average of 4–14 0–2.5 nm, 2–9 nm and 2–7 nm, respectively. These could verify the compatibility of all the currently synthesized compounds as CQDs proveniences for successive implantation of CQDs under solvothermal conditions.

On the other side, optical characters were reported to be attributed to the decoration of CQDs surface with heteroatom-containing functional groups [25,42]. The optical sensitivity of CQDs is mainly dependent on the surface states, decorated with heteroatom containing groups [23,25,42]. Mostly, it could be declared that, the photoluminescence is a reflect of the synergetic hybridization of the carbon core and the decorative functional groups [23,25,40,42–44]. Therefore, the optical characters of the currently implanted CQDs are owing to the heteroatom-containing decorative groups inborn from TM, A, S & T compounds, via overlapping of π-π* and n-π* transitions for the aromatic implanted CQDs. Moreover, the surface decoration of CQDs with heteroatom-containing functional groups is reflected in the absorption, leading to successive ingraining of colored CQDs to endow the possibility for their colorimetric detection.

Photoluminescence of the currently prepared CQDs was investigated via FL spectroscopic analysis and the FL emission spectral data after excitation at 370 nm were presented in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a represents FL emission spectral data for the as-synthesized TM, A, S & T compounds, whereas, Fig. 7b shows FL emission spectra of the implanted QDs; TM-CQDs, T-CQDs, A-CQDs & S-CQDs. From the plotted data it could be declared that, T compound was shown with the highest emission intensity compared to the others. After exploitation of all the synthesized compounds as CQDs proveniences, the emission intensity was estimated to be extremely higher, to follow the trend of; T-CQDs ˃ A-CQDs ˃ TM-CQDs ˃ S-CQDs, whereas, CQDs implanted from T compound was shown with the highest emission and the intensity was multiplied for eight times.

1HNMR (Supplementary data) of CQDs prepared from TM shows that, characteristic bands were detected for protons on the carbon atom that is a neighbor to phenyl group at 2.48 ppm, protons bonded to carbonyl group at 2.87 ppm, and aromatic or Sp2 CH = CH protons and CO–NH2 protons at 6.85–7.63 ppm. From 1HNMR spectrum for S-CQDs, specific bands that are corresponding to, protons on the carbon atom next to the aryl group, protons bonded to carbonyl group, the aromatic or Sp2 CH = CH protons & CO–NH2 protons & protons of hydroxyl decorative groups, were detected at 2.46–2.94 ppm, 3.20–3.32 ppm, 7.29–7.70 ppm and at 8.24–8.25 ppm, respectively. Whereas, for T-CQDs shows that, protons of carbon atom adjacent to carboxylic group & protons of carboxylic acid groups were detected at 2–3 ppm & 12.6–12.8 ppm, respectively.

Fig. 8 is plotted for 13CNMR spectral data for the nucleated CQDs in order to show that, for TM-CQDs (Fig. 8a), except, signals attributed to the cyclic thioxo group (CS) and that is corresponding to Sp2 carbons, all of the characteristic peaks of TM were persisted after its exploitation in ingraining of CQDs with slight red shifting, owing to fragmentation and oxidation of TM under solvothermal conditions for successive exploitation as CQDs provenience. Fig. 8b for A-CQDs shows that, the typical bands of CQDs were detected at 39.95–40.29 ppm, 98.74–107.19 ppm, 129.24–130.74 ppm, and at 159.64–120.29 ppm, which were corresponded to Sp3 carbon, carbon of CO, carbons in aromatic nuclei, CC aromatic or Sp2 carbons, respectively. Whereas, for S-CQDs, signals for CC aromatic or Sp 2 carbon atoms were obviously estimated (Fig. 8c). Spectral map of T-CQDs also shows that, all of the characteristic bands for T compound were completely disappeared.

Fig. 9 a represents the FT-IR spectra of CQDs prepared from the synthesized CQDs proveniences, so as it could be obviously observed that, the characteristic absorption bands for TM (two (NH), (CN), (CO) groups), A (NH & CN groups), S (CN group), T (CN group) were completely disappeared after its exploitation as generators of CQDs, that could be attributed to, their fragmentation and oxidation under the solvothermal conditions for nucleation of the requirable CQDs. Additionally, all of the prepared CQDs samples are exhibited with the significant band of graphite at 1636 cm−1 assigning for CC stretching. Whereas, compared to IR spectra of CQDs proveniences, shifting in CO band could be attributed to their oxidation for ingraining CQDs. Moreover, the characteristic bands of CQDs were observably detected in the spectrum of CQDs ingrained from compound T (T-CQDs) at 3161 cm−1 signed for “=CH, stretching”, 2924 cm−1 and 2055 cm−1 correspond to “CH, stretching” and “CO, conjugated”, 1142 cm−1 characteristic for “COC, stretching”.

Fig. 9b shows XRD data that were estimated for CQDs ingrained from the CQDs proveniences to declare that, compared to XRD data of TM, A, S & T compounds, and except S compound, it could be obviously shown that, the characteristic bands of CQDs proveniences were disappeared and new peaks were observed, to approve CQDs as more regulated/highly organized structure compared to their proveniences. Moreover, no specialized diffraction bands were estimated in case of TM-CQDs and S-CQDs, which reflected the amorphous nature of the synthesized CQDs from TM and S. For A-CQDs, the diffraction band at 2θ° = 25.1° was disappeared and new band at 2θ° = 24.1° was notified, related to variety in crystalline degree. In case of T-CQDs the diffraction patterns were not significantly changed, while small shifted for bands was observed.

Sulforhodamine B Assay (SRB Assay) depends on the stoichiometric bond of Sulforhodamine B dye with proteins under the mild acidic conditions and its consequent extraction under the basic medium. The amount of the dye extracted is a proxy for mass of cells, i.e., the number of cells in the tested sample. In the current approach, SRB Assay was performed for comparative study for the anti-proliferative effects of CQDs versus CQDs proveniences (TM, A, S &T), against MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cell line. Through SRB assay, the higher is the cytotoxicity of the prepared CQDs, the lower is the number of the counted viable cells, which in turn reflected in, lesser amounts of produced SBR dye and consequently, decrement in the detected absorbance. Therefore, monitoring the decrement of absorbance could be expressed as a key factor for following up the decrement in the number of viable cells.

Fig. 10 a represents the relation between the absorbance and the concentration of the synthesized CQDs proveniences (TM, A, S &T) and the plotted data obviously represents that, regardless to the type of the applied compound, the absorbance was lowered with the increment the concentration of the exploited compound, however, the most significant decrement in the detected absorbance was shown via the exploitation of compound A as antitumor drug with increment of concentration from 10 up to 100 μg/mL. Fig. 10b shows the effect of concentration for CQDs on the absorbance, while, the plotted data declared that, all of the prepared CQDs exhibited significantly higher effect in diminishing the detected absorbance values compared to their proveniences. The most significant decrement in the absorbance values was observably detected by exploitation of CQDs ingrained from TM by increment its concentration from 10 up to 100 μg/mL.

Fig. 10c shows the relation between the concentration of CQDs proveniences (TM, A, S & T) and the cell viability percentage, as compound A showed the highest mortality effect against the tested cancer cells, whereas, the cell viability percent of MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cells was significantly decreased from 70 % to 3 % with the increment of the concentration of A compound from 10 μg/mL up to 100 μg/mL. The estimated data in Fig. 10d & 10e, it could be declared that, the anti-proliferative activity of the implanted CQDs follows the trend of S-CQDs < T-CQDs < A-CQDs < TM-CQDs, whereas, using 100 μg/mL leaded to significant decrement of the cell viability percent to be 60 % (IC50, 121.2 μg/mL), 50 % (IC50, 86.4 μg/mL), 20 % (IC50, 36.1 μg/mL) & 17 % (IC50, 38.16 μg/mL), respectively. Meanwhile, CQDs ingrained from both TM (TM-CQDs) & A compound (A-CQDs) showed to exhibit the highest anti-proliferative activity against MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cell line compared to the others.

Inflammation is reported to be as a result of a complex mechanism of defense that takes a series of steps that might or might not be succeeded in removing the root that causing the tissue damaging alongside the sequential effects [45,46]. When the normal cell interacts with the pro-inflammatory drug “i.e., stressor”, and according to the type of the stressor, the cell is signaled for releasing different inflammatory mediators [46]. The most commonly preferable pro-inflammatory mediators are PAMPs (pathogen-associated molecular pattern molecules) and DAMPs (damage-associated molecular pattern molecules) secreted by the healthy cells as a result of exposure to pathogens and toxic chemicals, respectively. Such molecules are consequently involved in initiating the inflammatory pathway such as activation of DNA or RNA as well as modification of proteins and bacterial lipopolysaccharides [46].

The injured tissue elicits the macrophages, leading to produce the pro-inflammatory and inflammatory mediators, such as, nitric oxide synthase (NOS), inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) and cyclooxygenase (COX-2) [47]. The process starts with activating of the macrophages by tissue injuring that increasing the amount of NO, iNOS, COX-2, PGE2 (prostaglandin E2) and LPS (lipopolysaccharide) [48,49]. This could be mostly correlated to macrophages involvement in regulating certain pathways, like, MAPK pathway (mitogen-activated protein kinase) and NF-κB pathway (nuclear factor-kappa B) [49,50]. In event of blood vessels damaging, hemostatic mechanism kicks in reducing the blood flowing to the injured sites via the mechanical vasculature movements to initiate the blood clotting [51].

One of the sequential effects of inflammation is the denaturation of proteins [9]. Whereas, the denatured protein contributes in increasing the immune cells at the inflammation site and consequently, the immune cells share to change the acute inflammation to chronic inflammation [9,52]. Moreover, the development of cancer cells (Tumorigenesis), is also reported to be resulted from the chronic inflammation [53]. Transforming of acute inflammation to chronic inflammation only proceeds when the mechanisms that are responsible for terminating the acute inflammation are poorly controlled and the clearance of inflammatory mediators failed [54]. Subsequently, the inflammatory mediators continuously rise, leading to produce reactive oxygen (ROS such as O2 ., OH., 1O2, and H2O2) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS such as NO., ONOO–) that act in damaging of DNA [54,55]. Excess of ROS & RNS could act in the oxidation of biomolecules and modification of the proteins and genes, that could result in progressing of the inflammation to chronic inflammation [56]. Therefore, speeded sweeping of ROS and RNS is essential to inhibit the progression of inflammation.

In the current approach, Griess assay was performed for comparative study of the anti-inflammatory performance for CQDs versus CQDs proveniences (TM, A, S &T) via inhibition of nitrous oxide radicals (NO.) as one of the harmful RNS that mainly acted in progression of inflammation. Through Griess assay, the higher is the anti-inflammatory effects, the higher is the inhibition percent of nitrous oxide radicals (NO inhibition%). Fig. 11 a represented the relation between the NO inhibition% and the concentration of the synthesized CQDs proveniences and the plotted data obviously declared that, even at low concentrations, all of the synthesized CQDs proveniences were shown with high NO inhibition percent, as it was estimated to be 40 %, 59 % & 88 %, using 10 μM of TM, A & S, respectively. Regardless to the type of the applied compound, NO inhibition% was increased with increasing the concentration of the exploited compound. However, the most significant increment in NO inhibition% was shown via the exploitation of S compound as anti-inflammatory drug, as it was estimated to be 88 % to 97 % by increment of concentration from 10 up to 100 μM. Fig. 11b shows the effect of concentration for CQDs on NO inhibition%, while, the figured-out data clarified that, all of the prepared CQDs exhibited with lower NO inhibition% compared to their proveniences. However, CQDs ingrained from TM (TM-CQDs) were detected with the highest NO inhibition% by increment its concentration from 10 up to 100 μM to be 40 % to 89 %, respectively. Meanwhile, CQDs ingrained from TM showed to exhibit the highest anti-inflammatory performance compared to the others.

The previous studies in literature reported that, carbon-based structures showed high potentiality for application in manufacturing of vaccines, immune-therapeutical laborers, and immune-suppressive reagents [57]. CQDs, were reported to exhibit high affinity as anti-inflammatory reagents, so CQDs could be exploited as a potential therapeutical reagent for treatment of inflammatory diseases [58]. The mechanism of anti-inflammatory action of the prepared CQDs could be briefly explained according to literature as follows; according to literature [59], CQDs could act as sweepers for ROS & RNS via the delocalization of electron density in the conjugative aromatic structure; hence, they could be able to decrease the oxidative stress. Moreover, CQDs are composed of surface decorative functional groups, that could provoke low immune responses [60].

Herein, a comparative study is proposed to show the effect of variation in the heteroatoms in the main skeleton of CQDs proveniences, for their affinity as anti-inflammatory and anticancer drugs. Three heterocyclic compounds namely; “2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-6,11-dihydro-4H-pyrimido[2,1-b] quinazoline-3-carbonitrile” (compound A), “2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3]oxazine-3-carbonitrile” (compound S) and “2-(2,5-dimethoxyphenyl)-4,6-dioxo-4H,6H-benzo[e]pyrimido[2,1-b][1,3] thiazine-3-carbonitrile” (compound S), were successively ingrained from heterocyclic based CQDs sprout (target molecule, TM) via its treatment with anthranilic acid, salicylic acid and thio-salicylic acid, respectively. Both of CQDs proveniences and CQDs were all examined for their affinity as anti-inflammatory drugs via Griess assay, whereas, CQDs ingrained from TM showed to exhibit the highest anti-inflammatory performance compared to the others. Moreover, their anti-tumor performance was approved via sulforhodamine B assay. Whereas, CQDs ingrained from both TM (TM-CQDs) & A compound (A-CQDs) exhibited with the highest anti-proliferative activity against MCF-7: breast Adenocarcinoma cell line compared to the others.
